title: The last old market versus a car park
summary: Argue whether a town's last surviving traditional market should be demolished to build a multi-storey car park, using present perfect, conditionals, and opinion language.
topic: Should a town demolish its last old market to build a car park?
prompt: Write 3 paragraphs (400–500 words) arguing whether a small town should demolish its last surviving traditional open-air market to build a multi-storey car park. The town council says parking is the main problem; local traders say the market brings people into the centre. Choose a clear position, support it with a concrete reason, and use present perfect, a conditional, and 'although' or 'however' at least once each.
hintStarters: The market has been the heart of this town for generations, and in that time it has…
Although the parking problem is real, demolishing the market would be…
If the town were to invest in a shuttle service from the edge of town, drivers would…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lượng khách đi bộ qua lại
tạo ra hoạt động kinh tế
làm sang trọng hóa một khu vực
sơ đồ đậu xe và đi xe buýt
hồi sinh trung tâm thị trấn
làm suy yếu bản sắc của
năng lực đậu xe dư thừa

hintVocab.phrase: an anchor for the high street

hintVocab.meaningVi: cạnh tranh với bán lẻ ngoài thị trấn
làm rỗng ruột trung tâm thị trấn

sampleText:
The market in Hay-on-Wye has run every Thursday and Saturday for at least a hundred and forty years. In that time it has been the main reason many people from the surrounding villages bother to come into town at all. The council has now proposed demolishing the covered stalls to build a four-hundred-space multi-storey car park, on the grounds that parking is the single biggest complaint it receives from visitors. The proposal is understandable on its surface, but it is based on a mistaken reading of why people come here.

The problem with replacing the market with a car park is that it removes the reason the parking is needed. People drive into Hay-on-Wye on market days because the market draws them. If there were no market, there would be significantly less demand for the additional spaces in the first place. Several market towns in the Welsh Borders have tried the same logic — reducing the market to gain space for cars — and the result in each case has been a gradual hollowing out of the high street, as the foot traffic the market generated disappears and the remaining shops lose their customers. A car park without a destination is just an empty concrete structure. The council should, if it genuinely wants to address parking, investigate a park-and-ride scheme from the edge of town, which several comparable towns have introduced at relatively low cost. The traders have proposed exactly this solution and have been consistently ignored. That refusal to engage with a practical alternative before reaching for demolition is worth noting.

Although some traders have admitted that the market is smaller than it was ten years ago, that is an argument for investment and promotion, not demolition. The market has survived a recession, a pandemic, and the arrival of online shopping. It has done so because it offers something neither a website nor a supermarket can replicate: a reason to be in a particular place on a particular morning, among people you might recognise. That function does not show up easily in a traffic survey, but it is what holds a town centre together. Demolishing it to solve a parking problem would be like removing a building's load-bearing wall because you want more space in one room. The room would be bigger briefly, and then the ceiling would come down. The traders know this. It is puzzling that the council does not.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Present perfect to establish a long-standing situation
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'The market in Hay-on-Wye has run every Thursday and Saturday for at least a hundred and forty years' dùng hiện tại hoàn thành với 'for' để cho thấy tình trạng này bắt đầu trong quá khứ xa và vẫn còn tiếp diễn — đây là nền tảng của lập luận bảo tồn.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'The market in Hay-on-Wye has run every Thursday and Saturday for at least a hundred and forty years' uses the present perfect with 'for' to show that the situation began far in the past and is still ongoing — this is the foundation of the preservation argument.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Second conditional to explore a hypothetical consequence
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'If there were no market, there would be significantly less demand for the additional spaces' dùng điều kiện loại 2 để chỉ ra rằng việc phá bỏ chợ (một kịch bản giả thuyết) sẽ không giải quyết được vấn đề mà hội đồng muốn giải quyết.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'If there were no market, there would be significantly less demand for the additional spaces' uses a second conditional to show that demolishing the market (a hypothetical scenario) would not solve the problem the council thinks it would solve.

sampleTranslationVi:
Chợ ở Hay-on-Wye đã hoạt động mỗi thứ Năm và thứ Bảy trong ít nhất một trăm bốn mươi năm. Trong thời gian đó, nó là lý do chính khiến nhiều người từ các làng xung quanh có động lực vào thị trấn. Hội đồng hiện đã đề xuất phá dỡ các gian hàng có mái che để xây một bãi đậu xe bốn trăm chỗ nhiều tầng, với lý do rằng đậu xe là phàn nàn lớn nhất mà họ nhận được từ du khách. Đề xuất này dễ hiểu trên bề mặt, nhưng nó dựa trên sự hiểu sai về lý do tại sao mọi người đến đây.

Vấn đề với việc thay thế chợ bằng bãi đậu xe là nó xóa bỏ lý do mà việc đậu xe là cần thiết. Mọi người lái xe vào Hay-on-Wye vào ngày chợ vì chợ thu hút họ. Nếu không có chợ, nhu cầu về các chỗ đậu xe bổ sung sẽ ít hơn đáng kể ngay từ đầu. Một số thị trấn chợ ở Welsh Borders đã thử cùng một logic — thu hẹp chợ để có chỗ cho xe ô tô — và kết quả trong mỗi trường hợp là sự rỗng ruột dần dần của phố chính, khi lượng khách đi bộ mà chợ tạo ra biến mất và các cửa hàng còn lại mất đi khách hàng. Một bãi đậu xe không có điểm đến chỉ là một công trình bê tông trống rỗng. Hội đồng nên, nếu thực sự muốn giải quyết vấn đề đậu xe, điều tra một sơ đồ đậu xe và đi xe buýt từ rìa thị trấn, mà một số thị trấn tương đương đã đưa vào với chi phí tương đối thấp.

Mặc dù một số thương nhân đã thừa nhận rằng chợ nhỏ hơn so với mười năm trước, đó là lập luận để đầu tư và quảng bá, không phải phá dỡ. Chợ đã vượt qua một cuộc suy thoái, một đại dịch và sự xuất hiện của mua sắm trực tuyến. Nó đã làm được như vậy vì nó cung cấp thứ mà cả trang web lẫn siêu thị đều không thể sao chép: một lý do để ở một nơi cụ thể vào một buổi sáng cụ thể, giữa những người bạn có thể nhận ra. Chức năng đó không dễ hiện ra trong một cuộc khảo sát giao thông, nhưng đó là thứ giữ cho trung tâm thị trấn gắn kết. Phá dỡ nó để giải quyết vấn đề đậu xe sẽ giống như tháo bỏ tường chịu lực của tòa nhà vì bạn muốn có thêm không gian trong một căn phòng.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses the present perfect correctly to describe a long-standing, ongoing activity?
options: The market has run every week for over a century.
The market has been run every week for over a century.
The market have run every week for over a century.
The market was running every week for over a century.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (has + past participle) with 'for' describes an ongoing situation. 'Has been run' would imply the passive — someone runs it — but 'run' here is intransitive. 'Have' doesn't agree with the singular subject. 'Was running' is past continuous, implying it no longer happens.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Run' as an intransitive verb (an event takes place) uses active present perfect. Check subject-verb agreement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a second conditional correctly to show a hypothetical consequence?
options: If there were no market, there would be far less demand for parking.
If there is no market, there would be far less demand for parking.
If there were no market, there will be far less demand for parking.
If there would be no market, there would be far less demand for parking.
mcQuestions.explanation: Second conditional: if + past simple (were), main clause with would + base verb. 'Is' is present simple (first conditional). 'Will be' is future (first conditional). 'Would be … would' puts 'would' in both clauses, which is non-standard.
mcQuestions.hint: Second conditional = if + past simple, + would + base verb. 'Would' does not appear in the if-clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'on the grounds that' to introduce a reason?
options: The council proposed demolition on the grounds that parking was the main problem.
The council proposed demolition in the grounds that parking was the main problem.
The council proposed demolition on the ground that parking was the main problem.
The council proposed demolition on grounds of that parking was the main problem.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'On the grounds that' is the correct fixed phrase for giving a formal reason. 'In the grounds' is wrong. 'On the ground that' (singular) is less standard than 'on the grounds that'. 'On grounds of that' combines two different patterns incorrectly.
mcQuestions.hint: This is a fixed formal expression — only one version is standard.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'although' correctly to concede a weakening point?
options: Although the market is smaller than before, that is no reason to demolish it.
Although the market is smaller than before but that is no reason to demolish it.
Although, the market is smaller than before that is no reason to demolish it.
The market is smaller than before, although that is no reason to demolish it.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Although + subordinate clause, + main clause'. No 'but' after the comma. No comma directly after 'although'. The last option reverses the rhetorical order and weakens the argument's structure.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Although' concedes the weaker point first; the stronger counter-argument follows with no 'but'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option most naturally describes the gradual decline of a town centre's shops?
options: a gradual hollowing out of the high street
a gradual emptying of the high street
a gradual voiding of the high street
a gradual vacuuming of the high street
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Hollowing out' is the established metaphor for a town centre that loses its commercial activity while keeping its physical structure. 'Emptying' is possible but less vivid and less idiomatic. 'Voiding' is legal/formal. 'Vacuuming' is not used in this figurative sense.
mcQuestions.hint: Which metaphor most naturally describes a town that looks intact but has lost its commercial life?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a modal to make a recommendation?
options: The council should investigate a park-and-ride scheme instead.
The council should to investigate a park-and-ride scheme instead.
The council should investigating a park-and-ride scheme instead.
The council should investigated a park-and-ride scheme instead.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Should' is a modal and is always followed by the bare infinitive. 'Should to investigate' adds an incorrect 'to'. 'Should investigating' uses a gerund. 'Should investigated' uses a past participle — that would require 'should have' for a past context.
mcQuestions.hint: Modal + bare infinitive: no 'to', no '-ing', no past participle.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a relative clause to describe foot traffic?
options: The foot traffic that the market generates disappears when the market closes.
The foot traffic which the market generates it disappears when the market closes.
The foot traffic what the market generates disappears when the market closes.
The foot traffic the market generates them disappears when the market closes.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'That' is the correct relative pronoun for a defining relative clause about a thing. 'Which' is also acceptable but 'that + pronoun' repeats the object unnecessarily. 'What' cannot be used as a relative pronoun after a noun. 'Them' creates a pronoun clash.
mcQuestions.hint: In a defining relative clause about a thing, 'that' is correct. Don't add a pronoun inside the clause.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence most naturally uses 'hold together' to describe social cohesion?
options: The market is what holds the town centre together.
The market is what keeps the town centre together.
The market is what glues the town centre together.
The market is what maintains the town centre together.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Hold together' is the most natural metaphor for what maintains the social and commercial coherence of a space. 'Keep together' is possible but less idiomatic in this specific context. 'Glue together' is informal and vivid but slightly overblown. 'Maintain together' is non-standard — 'maintain' does not collocate with 'together' this way.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrasal verb most naturally describes what keeps a community space unified and functional?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'offer' with a direct object and a relative clause?
options: The market offers something that neither a website nor a supermarket can replicate.
The market offers something that neither a website nor a supermarket can replicates.
The market offers something what neither a website nor a supermarket can replicate.
The market offers something neither a website nor a supermarket can to replicate.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'That' introduces the relative clause correctly. 'Can replicates' conjugates the base verb after a modal. 'Something what' uses 'what' incorrectly — 'that' is the relative pronoun. 'Can to replicate' inserts 'to' incorrectly after a modal.
mcQuestions.hint: Check the relative pronoun after 'something' and the form of the verb after 'can'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses the present perfect with 'have survived' to describe resilience?
options: The market has survived a recession, a pandemic, and changes in shopping habits.
The market survived a recession, a pandemic, and changes in shopping habits.
The market have survived a recession, a pandemic, and changes in shopping habits.
The market has been surviving a recession, a pandemic, and changes in shopping habits.
mcQuestions.explanation: Present perfect (has + past participle) links past challenges to the present fact that the market still exists. Simple past 'survived' would suggest the story is over. 'Have' doesn't agree with singular 'market'. 'Has been surviving' is continuous — less natural for discrete events like 'a recession'.
mcQuestions.hint: Present perfect links past challenges to the current result (the market still exists). Check subject-verb agreement.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a simile correctly to explain a flawed decision?
options: Demolishing the market to solve parking would be like removing a load-bearing wall for more space.
Demolishing the market to solve parking would be as removing a load-bearing wall for more space.
Demolishing the market to solve parking would be like remove a load-bearing wall for more space.
Demolishing the market to solve parking will be like removing a load-bearing wall for more space.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Would be like + gerund' is the standard structure for a hypothetical comparison. 'As removing' is not standard — 'like' introduces the simile with a gerund. 'Like remove' uses the base form incorrectly. 'Will be like' shifts to a real future, but the context is conditional/hypothetical.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Be like' in a comparison takes a gerund ('-ing' form), not a base infinitive.

criticalThinkingQuestion: The sample argues that a market provides a 'reason to be in a place' that cannot be replicated by online shopping or a supermarket. Do you agree that physical gathering spaces have a social function beyond buying and selling? Can you give an example from your own city?
